Leaders Reach Budget Deal in New Jersey
Saying that they had ironed out differences that had threatened to plunge New Jersey into a fiscal and political crisis, Democratic legislators reached broad agreement Thursday night on a $28 billion budget that would give away more than $1 billion in property tax rebates and take in at least $130 million in new taxes.     The deal, announced by Acting Gov. Richard J. Codey and more than 20 members of the Senate and General Assembly, came hours before a legal deadline to pass the budget. But because many details had not yet been worked out, pending discussions on Friday by committees, the budget cannot be formally adopted until Saturday, at the earliest. That will make this budget the tardiest in the state's history, even though the governor's office, the Senate and the Assembly are controlled by one party.

If Interest Rates Turn Upside Down, Look Out
ALAN GREENSPAN, the chairman of the Federal Reserve, is upbeat -- but still worried. Inflation, energy prices and stubbornly low longer-term interest rates top his list of ''significant uncertainties'' about the economic outlook.     The important question for investors is how these uncertainties will influence the central bank's course, with the federal funds rate, its benchmark short-term interest rate, having already climbed 2.25 points, to 3.25 percent, since June 2004.

Japan Posts Slower Growth Than Expected
Japan's economy expanded at a slower-than-expected 1.1 percent annual rate in the April-June quarter, a report released on Friday showed. But economists said healthy capital spending and consumption, along with a brighter picture for exports, suggested that a sustained recovery was finally under way.     Gross domestic product grew 0.3 percent, adjusted for inflation, in the April-June period compared to the previous quarter, government data showed, less than economists' forecasts for a 0.5 percent rise.

Consumer Confidence Bounced Back in May
Consumer confidence unexpectedly rebounded in May after declining in April, as worries eased about the economy and jobs, the Conference Board said yesterday. ECONOMIC VIEW; The Crime: Slow Job Growth. A Suspect: Enron.
WHILE the economy has enjoyed steady growth and low inflation since the recession ended in the fall of 2001, many companies have been reluctant to add new workers. ''Any way you slice the data, employment growth has been disappointing in this recovery,'' said Lawrence F. Katz, professor of economics at Harvard. The Economic Policy Institute, a liberal research group in Washington, concludes that the lag in job growth has caused an ''employment deficit'' in the United States of 3.2 million jobs.      Mystified economists have pointed to various possible culprits: outsourcing, competition from China, high health care costs and lower work-force participation, to name a few. But there's one force that so far has managed to avoid blame for the sluggish pace of job growth: Enron.

Budget Office Sees Big City Deficits Ahead and No Plan Yet
The current surge in tax revenues will not continue long enough to help the city close multibillion-dollar deficits projected for the near future, and Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg has offered no plan for how to deal with the looming crisis, according to a new report by the city's Independent Budget Office. Japan's Spending Habit Roils Plan to Sell Big Bank
Red and white loading cranes loomed like steel sentinels over an empty wharf on a recent afternoon here. Capable of unloading shipping containers at a rate of almost one a minute, these computerized towers often stand frozen for days at a time for lack of ships.      Kobe's ranking in world container traffic tumbled to 43rd place last year, from 4th in 1980. Though Kobe's importance as a port started waning in the early 1990's, government bureaucrats responded to the 1995 earthquake here by renovating and expanding the port -- to the tune of $50 billion.
